content:
Introduction
Every Linux user has typed:
cat file.txtor:
lsor:
cp source.txt destination.txtwithout thinking about what actually happens underneath.
Question:
How does Linux know whether:
file.txtis stored on:
ext4
XFS
Btrfs
NFS
FAT32
USB Drive
RAM Disk?
How can the exact same command work on all of them?
Why doesn’t:
catneed separate code for:
ext4
xfs
ntfs
nfs
procfs
sysfs?
The answer is:
VFS (Virtual File System)
One of the most elegant abstractions inside the Linux kernel.
Today we’ll learn:
What VFS is
Why Linux needs VFS
How file operations work
VFS architecture
Inodes, Dentries, and Superblocks
Hands-on labs using /proc, /sys, mount, and strace
How Linux supports dozens of filesystems
Let’s dive in.
